Red = exam-style emphasis Green = stimulatory / normal Orange = nucleus / key contrast Purple = DHT / 5a-reductase Blue = structural / pathway cue
Johnson diction + HTML layout

Endocrine, reproductive steroids, and CAH

This page keeps the actual Johnson wording front and center, but packages it in a cleaner study layout: section cards, color-coded emphasis, clearer tables, and click-to-expand figures with a smoother lightbox. The goal is to make it feel like Johnson’s slides got turned into a study site without losing the way he phrases things.

Built from Intro to Endocrinology, Overview of Reproductive Steroids, Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia, and the supplemental note packet.
Design choice Endocrine sections keep a cooler blue/teal feel, reproductive steroid sections keep the warmer gold feel, and CAH sections pivot to a cleaner blue clinical look.
Figure behavior Every figure card opens in a lightbox. Use + and - for zoom, or pinch to zoom. When zoomed in, drag to pan.

Endocrine foundations

Start with the exact framing Johnson uses: what endocrine glands are, what hormones are made of, and the major ways endocrine signals act.

Johnson wording
  • Endocrine glands are traditionally defined as ductless glandular structures that release their hormonal secretions into the extracellular space where they can eventually access circulating plasma.
  • Classic endocrine glands include organs like the pituitary gland, thyroid gland, parathyroid glands, pancreatic islets, adrenal glands, ovaries, and testes.
  • It is now clear that hormones can be secreted from nontraditional endocrine organs and play critical roles in the regulation of physiological homeostasis. Examples include the heart, kidney, adipose tissue, bone, and gut.
  • Hormones vary widely in terms of their chemical composition. Specific examples include proteins, peptides, monoamines, amino acid derivatives, steroids, and lipids.
  • In general, protein, peptide, monoamine, and lipophilic hormones tend to exert their effects primarily through protein receptors at the cell membrane, while thyroid hormone and steroids tend to operate in the cell nucleus.
Color emphasis carried into HTML
  • Ductless glandular structures and extracellular space stay highlighted because that is the definition anchor.
  • The nontraditional endocrine organs were left color-coded because they are easy pick-off details.
  • The membrane-versus-nucleus contrast is preserved with cell membrane versus cell nucleus.
Signal modes from the figure Hormones can act on the cell that made them (intracrine), the next cell over (paracrine), or travel through the blood (endocrine). Neurotransmitters can also spill into the extracellular compartment and behave as paracrine or endocrine regulators.

The hypothalamo-pituitary axis

Johnson’s pituitary sequence is very anatomical: lobe origin, route from the hypothalamus, major hormones, then the vascular reason why the portal system matters.

Johnson wording
  • The pituitary is divided into two lobes: the anterior lobe (adenohypophysis) and the posterior lobe (neurohypophysis).
  • Anterior lobe derived from Rathke’s pouch. Posterior lobe derived from neuroectoderm.
  • The anterior lobe produces and releases several hormones that regulate other endocrine glands and bodily functions: GH, PRL, ACTH, TSH, LH, and FSH.
  • The posterior lobe releases two hormones: oxytocin and vasopressin (ADH).
  • The anterior pituitary is connected indirectly to the hypothalamus via the hypophyseal portal system.
  • The posterior pituitary is connected directly to the hypothalamus via the hypothalamo-hypophyseal tract.
  • The portal vessels that bring hypothalamic hormones directly to the anterior lobe are critical, because these hormones would not exist in high enough concentrations to induce anterior pituitary hormone effects if released directly into the general circulation.

Feedback language to keep

  • Negative feedback loops more common than positive feedback loops.
  • Long loop = final product hormone inhibits its own production.
  • Short loop = pituitary hormones inhibit their own product and regulate their own secretion.
  • Most endocrine diseases occur at one of three levels: tertiary (hypothalamus), secondary (pituitary), or primary (peripheral gland).
Why ADH and oxytocin are neurohormones: neurons from the hypothalamus extend directly into the posterior pituitary, releasing hormones there, which are then picked up by venous drainage and distributed into peripheral blood.

Pituitary wording

  • Hypothalamus releases gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH), which stimulates the anterior pituitary.
  • Anterior Pituitary (Adenohypophysis) releases FSH and LH.
  • Peripheral gland hormones feed back to prevent excessive hormone release.

Receptor-location contrast

  • Steroids do not bind receptors on the cell surface, but diffuse into cells and bind to intracellular receptors.
  • Thyroid hormone receptors are part of the nuclear receptor superfamily and influence gene expression by binding DNA elements as dimers.

HPG axis and testosterone synthesis / binding

The supplemental note packet fills in the testicular steroidogenesis details that sit very naturally beside Johnson’s reproductive steroid slides.

Johnson + note-packet wording
  • Testosterone is synthesized in Leydig cells of the testes under the regulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis.
  • The hypothalamus secretes gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH).
  • GnRH stimulates the anterior pituitary to release luteinizing hormone (LH).
  • LH binds LH receptors on Leydig cells, activating the cAMP-PKA pathway, which upregulates steroidogenic enzymes.
  • Cholesterol is transported into the mitochondria via steroidogenic acute regulatory (StAR) protein.
  • Cholesterol → Pregnenolone in the inner mitochondrial membrane.
  • Pregnenolone diffuses to the smooth ER → Progesterone.
  • Progesterone → 17α-Hydroxyprogesterone → Androstenedione → Testosterone.
  • Testosterone is then released into the bloodstream, where it circulates in both free and bound forms.
  • 98% is bound to sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) and albumin; 2% is free and biologically active.

Binding to the androgen receptor

  1. Free testosterone diffuses across the plasma membrane into target cells.
  2. In some tissues, testosterone is converted to 5α-dihydrotestosterone (DHT) by 5α-reductase, which has a higher affinity for AR.
  3. Testosterone or DHT binds to cytoplasmic AR, inducing a conformational change.
When bound, the androgen receptor protein complex dissociates and results in activation of gene transcription, causing development of male reproductive organs, spermatogenesis, secondary sexual characteristics, and libido / mood regulation.
Hormone Source Target Mechanism of action Effect on testosterone synthesis
GnRH Hypothalamus Anterior pituitary Binds a GPCR in anterior pituitary gonadotrophs; phospholipase C stimulates inositol triphosphate and increased intracellular calcium. Promotes LH and FSH release; increased testosterone later feeds back to inhibit LH and GnRH release.
LH Anterior pituitary Leydig cells Binds LH receptors, activates cAMP-PKA signaling, increases StAR protein and steroidogenic enzymes. Directly stimulates testosterone production in Leydig cells.
FSH Anterior pituitary Sertoli cells Binds FSH receptors, promotes androgen-binding protein (ABP) and inhibin B secretion. Supports spermatogenesis by maintaining high intratesticular testosterone levels.

Negative feedback wording worth memorizing

  • Testosterone produced by Leydig cells is partly converted into 17β-estradiol.
  • Testosterone and 17β-estradiol feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary to inhibit further LH release and decrease the amplitude of LH pulses.
  • Sertoli cells secrete inhibin B in response to FSH stimulation.
  • Inhibin B specifically acts at the pituitary level to inhibit further FSH secretion.

Plasma binding proteins

  • Plasma binding proteins are made by the liver.
  • Hormones like steroids and thyroid hormones are lipid-soluble and bind plasma proteins.
  • Facilitates the transport of hormones through the bloodstream.
  • Protects hormones from degradation.
  • Bound hormones are inactive for binding unlike unbound hormones that can bind receptors on target cells.
  • Free hormones that bind albumin are known as bioavailable hormones.
Hormone Source Target Mechanism of Action Effect on Testosterone Synthesis
Gonadotropin-Releasing Hormone (GnRH) Hypothalamus Anterior Pituitary
  1. binds to a GPCR in the anterior pituitary gonadotrophs
    • Causes activation of phospholipase C
  2. Phospholipase C stimulates inositol triphosphate
    • Increased inositol triphosphate causes increased intracellular calcium and indirectly stimulates cAMP leading to release of LH and FSH
Release is stimulated by NE, Neuropeptide Y, and leptin
Release is inhibited by beta-endorphin, IL-1, GABA, and dopamine
Indirectly stimulates testosterone synthesis by promoting LH and FSH release
Increased testosterone levels inhibit LH and GnRH release from the anterior pituitary (negative feedback)
Luteinizing Hormone (LH) Anterior Pituitary Leydig Cells (Testes) Binds to LH receptors (LHR), activates cAMP-PKA signaling, increases StAR protein and steroidogenic enzymes. Directly stimulates testosterone production in Leydig cells
Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) Anterior Pituitary Sertoli Cells (Testes) Binds to FSH receptors (FSHR), promotes androgen-binding protein (ABP) and inhibin B secretion. Supports spermatogenesis by maintaining high intratesticular testosterone levels
GnRH-LH-FSH table with stimulation and inhibition notes preserved.
Regulatory Hormone Source Target Effect on Target Overall Impact
Testosterone Leydig cells Hypothalamus, Pituitary Inhibits GnRH (hypothalamus) and LH, FSH (pituitary) Reduces LH and FSH secretion, lowering testosterone production
Dihydrotestosterone (DHT) Leydig cells (via 5α-reductase) Hypothalamus, Pituitary Stronger inhibition of GnRH and LH Reinforces suppression of testosterone production
Estradiol Leydig cells, adipose tissue (via aromatase) Hypothalamus, Pituitary Inhibits GnRH and LH secretion Decreases testosterone synthesis
Inhibin B Sertoli cells Anterior Pituitary Selectively inhibits FSH secretion Reduces FSH stimulation of Sertoli cells, controlling spermatogenesis
Compact feedback table: testosterone, DHT, estradiol, and inhibin B.

Reproductive steroids, embryology, and DSD framing

This section keeps Johnson’s gold-slide wording and strips it into study chunks: classes of steroids, embryologic timing, duct-system differentiation, and external genital development.

Classes of steroids

  • Cortical steroids: cortisol (C21), aldosterone (C21)
  • Gonadal steroids: progestens / pregnanes (C21), androgens / androstanes (C19), estrogens / estranes (C18)
  • Steroids can be synthesized completely from acetate, or directly from the cholesterol molecule.
  • All of the enzymes required by one cell for synthesis of a specific steroid are usually contained within that cell in a biosynthetic package.

The mother of all steroids

  • The initial production of all C18, C19, and C21 steroids involves cleaving off the terminal 6 carbons of cholesterol in the mitochondria to form pregnenolone.
  • The enzyme responsible is mitochondrial cytochrome P450scc, also referred to as desmolase.
  • Cholesterol itself feeds back negatively on HMGCoA reductase to inhibit further cholesterol synthesis.
  • Statins work essentially by mimicking the action of cholesterol on HMGCoA reductase.

Which tissues make steroids?

  • The primary steroidogenic tissues are the male and female gonads, and the adrenal cortex.
  • Small amounts of in situ steroid synthesis also occur in the brain and skin because cytochrome P450scc is present there.
  • Clinically, distinguish between changes in primary synthesis and changes in peripheral conversion of one steroid to another.

Brief embryology review

  • Normally, genetic sex or genotype is established at the moment of fertilization.
  • If the fertilizing sperm contains a Y chromosome, the zygote will be XY and ultimately develop into a male embryo.
  • If the fertilizing sperm contains an X chromosome, the zygote will be XX and the sex of the zygote will be female.
  • Normal males and females derive their gonads from the same embryological tissue: the urogenital ridge.
  • In a normal genetic male, the SRY gene on the Y chromosome expresses around the 7th week and drives paired testes formation.
  • In the absence of a Y chromosome, ovaries begin developing around week 11.

Duct systems and external genitalia

  • XY males and XX females both initially have the Wolffian ducts and the Mullerian ducts.
  • Sertoli cells produce Mullerian-Inhibiting Substance (MIS), which causes regression of the Mullerian duct system.
  • Leydig-cell testosterone causes the Wolffian ducts to differentiate into the seminal vesicles, epididymis, vas deferens, and ejaculatory duct.
  • A genital tubercle, urogenital groove, and labioscrotal folds are present by 7 weeks.
  • Conversion of testosterone to the more potent androgen 5 alpha dihydrotestosterone by 5 alpha reductase type 2 is required for effective androgen-mediated external genital development in XY males.

Androgen insensitivity syndrome / CAIS

Johnson’s wording here is very definition-heavy, so the best move is to keep it nearly verbatim and then set the contrasts side by side.

  • Androgen insensitivity syndrome (AIS) is typically characterized by evidence of feminization of the external genitalia at birth, abnormal secondary sexual development in puberty, and infertility in male individuals with a normal 46,XY karyotype.
  • AIS represents a spectrum of defects in androgen action due to mutations in the androgen receptor gene.
  • Broad phenotypes at birth:
    • CAIS: 46 XY male born with normal appearing female external genitalia
    • PAIS: 46 XY male born with ambiguous genitalia
    • MAIS: 46 XY male with normal appearing male genitalia
  • The gene for the human androgen receptor is located within the Xq11-13 area of the X chromosome.
  • CAIS is a complete loss-of-function mutation in the androgen receptor gene in XY males.
  • Affected individuals have normal testes, normal production of testosterone, and normal conversion to DHT, which differentiates CAIS from 5-alpha reductase deficiency.
  • Because the testes produce normal MIS, affected individuals do not have fallopian tubes, a uterus, or a proximal upper vagina.
  • At puberty, androgens not taken up by androgen receptors may be aromatized to estrogens in other tissues, including breast tissue.
Quick clinical distinction: In CAIS, the testes still make testosterone, DHT, and MIS. The receptor is the problem, so external genitalia can appear female while Mullerian structures regress.

Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH)

This is the section where Johnson’s slide language and the strongest color-coded emphasis line up almost perfectly, so both are preserved directly.

Definition and mechanism
  • Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) encompasses a group of autosomal recessive disorders involving a deficiency of enzymes required for synthesis of cortisol, aldosterone, or both.
  • Defective conversion of 17-hydroxyprogesterone to 11-deoxycortisol by 21-hydroxylase accounts for about 95% of all cases.
  • enzyme deficiencies in the adrenal steroid synthesis pathway — specifically 21-Hydroxylase (21-OH)
  • This prevents conversion of progesterone and 17-hydroxyprogesterone into their respective cortisol and aldosterone precursors.
  • The result is lack of cortisol and aldosterone with excess production of androgens.
  • Chronic ACTH stimulation causes the adrenal glands to enlarge, which explains the hyperplasia.
Terminology + phenotype
  • Johnson’s updated 2024 phrasing: patients with 21-hydroxylase deficiency should be categorized as having either classic or non-classic CAH.
  • Classic disease may include adrenal insufficiency at birth; older terminology split it into salt-wasting and simple virilizing, but he notes those labels have fallen out of favor.
  • Non-classic CAH presents with signs of hyperandrogenism, without adrenal insufficiency.
  • Classic XX patients can have ambiguous external genitalia at birth, while classic XY patients typically have normal appearing external genitalia and may present later with virilization.

Why genitalia differ in XX vs XY classical CAH

  • Normal genetic males and females both have two duct systems early on.
  • Which duct system develops depends on whether testes are present, because Sertoli cells make MIS.
  • In XX females there are ovaries, not testes, so MIS is absent.
  • Johnson’s red-line point preserved: Patients with 46 XX classic CAH will have a normal cervix, uterus and ovaries at birth.
  • Excess adrenal androgens from the adrenal cortex can still fuse the urogenital groove and enlarge the clitoris, rendering ambiguous external genitalia at birth in these newly affected XX females.

Classic versus non-classic CAH

  • Classic CAH: cortisol severely reduced, aldosterone often deficient, androgen levels highly increased, virilization / salt-wasting / early virilization.
  • Non-classic CAH: cortisol mildly reduced, aldosterone usually normal, androgen levels moderately increased, later-onset hyperandrogenism without adrenal insufficiency.
table phrasing preserved: deficient (salt-wasting form) versus normal (Non salt-wasting), and Highly increased androgen levels in classic disease.
Aspect 21-hydroxylase deficiency (classic CAH) Non-classic CAH
Enzyme affected 21-hydroxylase 21-hydroxylase (partial deficiency)
Cortisol production Severely reduced Mildly reduced
Aldosterone production Often deficient (salt-wasting form) Usually normal (non salt-wasting)
Androgen levels Highly increased → virilization Moderately increased → mild hyperandrogenism
Clinical presentation Ambiguous genitalia in females, salt-wasting crisis, early virilization in males Late-onset hirsutism, irregular menses in women, hyperandrogenism without adrenal insufficiency

Screening and diagnosis

  • Serum 17-hydroxyprogesterone remains the gold standard for confirming a diagnosis of classic CAH in affected neonates.
  • In older children with symptoms, an early morning baseline 17-hydroxyprogesterone is helpful because it falls rapidly during the day.
  • An ACTH stimulation test is recommended to confirm the diagnosis.
  • A significantly elevated plasma 17-hydroxyprogesterone after ACTH is diagnostic for classic CAH.
  • non-classical will normally not be detectable by screening
  • GnRH injection can help distinguish CAH from normal early puberty in a hyperandrogenic male child because in CAH the HPG axis is not fully activated yet.

Treatment

  • All patients who have classic CAH due to 21-alpha hydroxylase deficiency receive long-term glucocorticoid or aldosterone replacement (or both).
  • Glucocorticoid Replacement
  • Mineralocorticoid Replacement in salt-wasting forms (for example, fludrocortisone) to restore aldosterone levels.
  • Another approach is combined glucocorticoid + mineralocorticoid, with an aromatase inhibitor and flutamide or bicalutamide (androgen receptor antagonists) to reduce virilization.
  • Johnson’s new-treatment slide: Crenessity (crinecerfont) was presented as acting by blocking CRF Type 1 receptor in the anterior pituitary, decreasing ACTH release.

High-yield CAH pull-outs

  • Will cause ambiguous genitalia in females but normal ovaries, uterus, and cervix at birth.
  • Normal genital development in males may still be early because of high testosterone.
  • Hyperandrogenism in boys can be pseudo-precocious puberty, since the source is adrenal rather than gonadal.

Color-coded emphasis dump

This appendix is here so the red / green / orange / purple exam cues still exist on the page in a fast-scroll format.

Endocrine + pituitary cues

  • ductless glandular structures
  • extracellular space
  • protein, peptide, monoamine, and lipophilic
  • through protein receptors at the cell membrane
  • thyroid hormone and steroids
  • cell nucleus
  • indirectly via hypophyseal portal system
  • directly via hypothalamo-hypophyseal tract
  • Final product hormone inhibits its own production
  • prevent excessive hormone release
  • Pituitary hormones inhibit their own product
  • regulate its own secretion
  • Steroids do not bind receptors on the cell surface
  • diffuse into cells and bind to intracellular receptors

HPG / testosterone cues

  • gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH)
  • luteinizing hormone (LH)
  • Leydig cells
  • Cholesterol → Pregnenolone
  • Progesterone → 17α-Hydroxyprogesterone → Androstenedione → Testosterone
  • bloodstream
  • sex hormone-binding globulin
  • 2% is free and is biologically active
  • Free testosterone
  • 5α-dihydotestosterone (DHT)
  • 5α-reductase
  • activation of gene transcription
  • promoting LH and FSH release
  • Increased testosterone levels inhibit LH and GnRH release
  • Release is stimulated by NE, Neuropeptide Y, and leptin
  • Release is inhibited by beta-endorphin, IL-1, GABA, and dopamine
  • Directly stimulates testosterone production in Leydig cells
  • maintaining high intratesticular testosterone levels
  • Testosterone and 17β-estradiol feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary
  • Inhibin B specifically acts at the pituitary level to inhibit further FSH secretion
  • Facilitates the transport of hormones through the bloodstream
  • Protects hormones from degradation
  • Bound hormones are inactive for binding
  • bioavailable hormones

CAH cues

  • sex hormones inhibit the release of GnRH, LH, and FSH
  • enzyme deficiencies in the adrenal steroid synthesis pathway
  • 21-Hydroxylase (21-OH)
  • excess production of androgens
  • Will cause ambiguous genitalia in females
  • Chronic ACTH stimulation causes the adrenal glands to enlarge
  • deficient (salt-wasting form)
  • normal (Non salt-wasting)
  • Highly increased
  • Patients with 46 XX classic CAH will have a normal cervix, uterus and ovaries at birth
  • rendering ambiguous external genitalia at birth in these newly affected XX females
  • not fully activated yet
  • gold standard for confirming classic CAH
  • early morning baseline 17-hydroxyprogesterone
  • long-term glucocorticoid or aldosterone replacement
  • aromatase inhibitor
  • androgen receptor antagonists
  • blocking CRF Type 1 receptor in the anterior pituitary
